description:
You are a backup-operator trainee who must prove that you can quickly analyse a raw 
    restore manifest by using nothing more than the standard column-manipulation tools 
    that ship with every GNU/Linux distribution.  
    A sample manifest has already been placed for you at  
        /home/user/backup_test/input/manifest.log  

    1. Create a new directory called  
           /home/user/backup_test/output  
       to hold every deliverable that this exercise asks for.

    2. From the manifest, build a comma-separated report named  
           /home/user/backup_test/output/restore_manifest.csv  

       a. The CSV file MUST start with **exactly** this header line (no leading spaces, 
          no extra columns, exactly one comma between names, followed by a single \n):  
              FILE,SIZE,STATUS  

       b. For every record that appears in manifest.log, append a line that contains 
          only columns 3, 4 and 5 (in that order) from the original file, converted 
          to CSV format.  
          • Column delimiter in the source file is one or more spaces.  
          • Column delimiter in the destination file is a single comma.  
          • Preserve the original ordering of the records.  
          • Do **not** add spaces before or after the commas in the CSV.  
          • The automated test will fail if any extra columns, blank lines or trailing 
            whitespace are present.

    3. Produce a second file called  
           /home/user/backup_test/output/summary.txt  

       This summary must list ONLY the paths of files whose STATUS column equals the 
       string “FAILED”, one path per line, sorted in ascending (lexicographic) order, 
       with no blank lines before, after or between the entries.

    4. Make sure both output files are world-readable (chmod 644 is fine).

    Hints you might consider (but are **not** required to use): 
       • cut ‑d and ‑f to select columns  
       • tr or sed to translate space delimiters to commas  
       • paste to merge independent column extractions  

    When you are finished, you can inspect your work with `cat`, but the automated 
    evaluation will do a byte-for-byte comparison, so watch for sneaky whitespace 
    errors!
truth:
#----------------------------------------------------
    #  Pre-existing file that the agent must read:
    #----------------------------------------------------
    File: /home/user/backup_test/input/manifest.log
    Permissions: 644

    Actual content (each line terminated with a single \n):
    2024-06-01 01:00:00 /var/www/html/index.html 2048 OK
    2024-06-01 01:00:05 /var/log/syslog 1048576 OK
    2024-06-01 01:00:10 /etc/passwd 4096 FAILED
    2024-06-01 01:00:15 /home/user/data/report.pdf 8192 OK
    2024-06-01 01:00:20 /home/user/secret/notes.txt 1234 FAILED

    #----------------------------------------------------
    #  Files that the agent must create:
    #----------------------------------------------------

    1. /home/user/backup_test/output/restore_manifest.csv
       Permissions: 644
       Expected content (each line terminated with a single \n):
       FILE,SIZE,STATUS
       /var/www/html/index.html,2048,OK
       /var/log/syslog,1048576,OK
       /etc/passwd,4096,FAILED
       /home/user/data/report.pdf,8192,OK
       /home/user/secret/notes.txt,1234,FAILED

    2. /home/user/backup_test/output/summary.txt
       Permissions: 644
       Expected content (each line terminated with a single \n):
       /etc/passwd
       /home/user/secret/notes.txt
